query: Gonadotropin-releasing Hormone (GnRH) and permissive action during puberty

retrieved_docs.title: Precocious Puberty -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The HPG axis then becomes dormant until its subsequent activation in adolescence. The pulsatile release of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) from the hypothalamus stimulates the secretion of follicle stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH) from the anterior pituitary gland. FSH and LH initiate spermatogenesis and the release of testosterone in males and oogenesis and the release of estradiol in females, respectively. Activation of the gonads is known as gonadarche.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone -- Development -- At Puberty:
retrieved_docs.content: This temporary pause in GnRH release ends at puberty, and recent studies have shown that Kisspeptin neurons are responsible for activation of hypothalamic-gonadotropic axis activation causing the GnRH release at puberty. Initially, at puberty, GnRH is released in low-frequency pulses during the night, but after the maturation of synaptic connections, it matches the adult pattern. In males, the GnRH pulses occur after 2 hours, while in a female, it changes according to the phases of the menstrual cycle. It is clear that the episodic release of GnRH is a general phenomenon. Also, fluctuation in the amplitude and frequency of GnRH bursts plays a vital role in initiating hormonal charges that ultimately regulate the menstrual cycle. The frequency of GnRH bursts is decreased by testosterone and progesterone and increased by estrogens. The frequency (1 pulse of GnRH/60 to 90 mins) during the late follicular phase of the menstrual cycle is increased, culminating in the LH surge. In the secretory phase of the menstrual cycle, the action of progesterone decreases the frequency (1 pulse of GnRH/200mins). At the end of the menstrual cycle, when progesterone and estrogen secretion decreases, the frequency increases. The sensitivity of gonadotropes increases significantly during the midcycle LH surge; this is due to the exposure to pulses of GnRH at a specific frequency, which describes a critical self-priming effect of GnRH that produces a maximum LH response. Thus, changes in GnRH frequency and amplitude alters the synthesis of gonadotropin synthesis and LH and FSH release.

retrieved_docs.title: Dwarfism -- Treatment / Management -- Hormonal Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) analogs are used to halt the progression of precocious puberty. When administered, these analogs continuously suppress the release of gonadotropins from the pituitary and further down the gonadal steroids, which were responsible for premature bone maturation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sexual Maturity Rating -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The onset of puberty in both males and females relies on the hypothalamus. The intermittent release of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) from the hypothalamus, also known as the hypothalamic pulse generator, signals the release of luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) from the anterior pituitary. [4] There is a belief that the neurotransmitters GABA and kisspeptin inhibit the release of GnRH. As an individual matures, the hypothalamus becomes less sensitive to this inhibition and eventually shifts towards pulsatile surges. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: GnRH is vital because it has implications in the pathogenesis of central hypogonadism. GnRH and its analogs are used as a treatment modality in infertility, endometriosis, central precocious puberty, and hormone-dependent malignancies like breast cancer and ovarian cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) is a crucial substance in the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis in humans. Production of GnRH occurs in the neurons of the hypothalamus and causes the downstream production of sex hormones by the gonads. This hormone ultimately regulates puberty onset, sexual development, and ovulatory cycles in females.  Intrinsic or extrinsic disruptions in this pathway can lead to the development of pathologic conditions in humans. Pharmacologic analogs of GnRH are useful in the treatment of gynecological disease due to their ability to block estrogen secretion from the ovary. Emerging evidence suggests that stimulation of tumor GnRH receptors induces antiproliferative and antimetastatic activity, making it a potential therapeutic target. [1] This review will discuss the cellular and genetic characteristics of GnRH as well as its physiologic and pathophysiologic mechanisms in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothalamic Hamartoma -- Treatment / Management -- Gonadotropin-releasing Hormone Agonists
retrieved_docs.content: Precocious puberty can usually be treated successfully with medications. Leuprolide acetate inhibits the release of GnRH that is required to trigger puberty. It is generally administered intramuscularly once monthly for the duration that puberty needs to be suppressed.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: GnRH or GnRH agonist binds to GnRH receptors located at pituitary cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone -- Development -- During Embryonic life:
retrieved_docs.content: The levels of GnRH gradually increase and reach a peak level at the mid gestational age, after which they gradually fall toward the end of the gestational period due to the negative feedback effect of circulating placental steroids. At birth development of GnRH neurons is complete, but the functional maturation of synaptic connectivity is attained later in life, especially at puberty. After birth, these levels remain elevated for two years in girls while for six months in boys. The mechanism of suppression of GnRH after birth is still unknown, but certain neurotransmitters like GABA and Neuropeptide Y seem to play an important role in the suppression of GnRH before puberty.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple feedback mechanisms in humans regulate GnRH activity and have implications on sexual development, breastfeeding, menstruation, and fertility. Prolactin is a hormone that exhibits inhibitory effects on GnRH, thus inhibiting FSH and LH production by the anterior pituitary. In females, this ovulatory inhibition leads to lactational amenorrhea, which acts as a physiologic contraceptive. In addition to lactation in females, prolactin inhibition of GnRH can result in infertility in males due to decreased spermatogenesis. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Puberty -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The hypothalamus releases GnRH in a pulsatile manner, which then stimulates the release of FSH and LH from the anterior pituitary gland. Prior to puberty, FSH and LH levels in the body are low. Approximately one year before the onset of puberty, CNS inhibition of GnRH subsides, leading to a rise in the release of FSH and LH. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin Inhibitor -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: GnIH binds and stimulates the GPR147 receptor to suppress adenylyl cyclase formation, ultimately suppressing gonadotrope function. The inhibitory effects of GnIH on reproduction are mainly accomplished at the hypothalamic-pituitary level. Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) neurons, gonadotropes, and the gonads are major targets of GnIH action based on the interaction and distribution of the GnIH receptor. [6] [12] Apart from being a negative regulator of reproductive behavior and inhibiting gonadotropins, GnIH also acts on kisspeptins (a group of peptide fragments encoded by the KISS1 gene in humans). [13] Kisspeptins are present in the lateral preoptic area and arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus and play a role in initiating the preovulatory GnRH/luteinizing hormone (LH) surge, which is crucial for ovulation. [14] Kisspeptins and GnIH are two neuropeptides of the hypothalamus. They have an essential role in the regulation of the reproductive axis. Kisspeptins are stimulators of the reproductive axis, while GnIH is the inhibitory opponent.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Phospholipase C breaks down PIP (Phosphoinositol diphosphate) into IP (inositol triphosphate) and DAG (Diacyl Glycerol).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Puberty -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) neurons of the hypothalamus control the initiation of puberty. The pulsatile secretion of GnRH by these neurons brings about the physiologic changes associated with puberty. Currently, there are increasing amounts of evidence showing that kisspeptin neurons in the arcuate nucleus release neurokinin B and dynorphin to generate the pulsatile secretion of GnRH. [2] GnRH causes the release of luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) from the gonadotropic cells of the anterior pituitary gland. FSH and LH affect the Leydig and Sertoli cells in the testes and the theca and granulosa cells of the ovary. The zona reticularis of the adrenal cortex produces the hormones responsible for adrenarche and function separately from the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2279
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist is classified as a hormone and related agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2955
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists constitute a class of compounds that stimulate the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone receptor to modulate pituitary hormone release, and triptorelin is a specific agent that belongs to this LHRH‑agonist class. Recognizing triptorelin as a member of the LHRH‑agonist group is essential for understanding its mechanism of action and therapeutic applications in hormone‑dependent conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1601
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol is classified as a hormone, reflecting its role as a key endogenous steroid that regulates reproductive physiology. Gonadotropiny, representing the family of gonadotropins, is a subclass within the broader hormone category, indicating that gonadotropins share fundamental hormonal properties. The group termed “Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain” includes estradiol as one of its members, situating estradiol among both naturally occurring and synthetically derived estrogenic compounds used in clinical practice. Choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are both identified as having the structural class gonadotropiny, meaning they belong to the gonadotropin family and share similar molecular architecture and biological functions related to stimulation of the gonads. Together, these relationships delineate how estradiol fits within the hormone hierarchy, how gonadotropins are organized as a hormonal subclass, and how specific agents such as choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are structurally related to the gonadotropin class, informing both pharmacologic classification and therapeutic application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1368
retrieved_docs.content: Sex hormones and modulators of the genital system constitute a therapeutic class that encompasses both gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants and combination formulations of progestogens and estrogens. Within this class, progestogens and estrogens in combination represent a distinct subgroup, and fixed combinations of progestogens and estrogens are a specific type of that subgroup.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1396
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist is classified as a gonadorelin acetate, indicating that it belongs to the chemical family of gonadorelin acetate compounds, and it is also identified as a member of the triptorelin group. This dual classification shows that LHRH agonists share the structural characteristics of gonadorelin acetate while being related to the synthetic peptide triptorelin, a specific GnRH analog used clinically to suppress gonadotropin secretion. Recognizing that Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist “is a” gonadorelin acetate and belongs to the triptorelin family aids clinicians in understanding its pharmacologic properties, therapeutic applications, and potential side‑effect profile when selecting hormonal therapies for patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2560
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists are classified within the broader category of hormones and related agents, reflecting their role in modulating the endocrine system. Within this class, triptorelin represents a specific member, exemplifying the pharmacologic application of LHRH agonists in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2719
retrieved_docs.content: Follitropin beta is classified within the structural class gonadotropiny, indicating that it is a member of the gonadotropin family of hormones that regulate reproductive function. This classification reflects its molecular similarity to other gonadotropins such as follicle‑stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH), and underlies its clinical use in assisted reproductive technologies to stimulate ovarian follicle development. Understanding that follitropin beta belongs to the gonadotropiny structural class helps clinicians predict its pharmacologic behavior, therapeutic indications, and potential interactions with other agents that affect the hypothalamic‑pituitary‑gonadal axis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 567
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants are classified as a subset of sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, indicating that these agents belong to the broader category of hormonal substances that regulate and modulate genital function and reproductive processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1464
retrieved_docs.content: Choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are both classified within the gonadotropiny structural class, indicating that they share a common protein architecture characteristic of gonadotropin hormones. As members of this structural class, both agents exhibit similar molecular features that enable them to interact with gonadal receptors and influence reproductive processes. Recognizing that choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta belong to the same gonadotropin family helps clinicians understand their comparable mechanisms of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and appropriate therapeutic uses in assisted reproduction and related endocrine treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 768
retrieved_docs.content: SEX HORMONES AND MODULATORS OF THE GENITAL SYSTEM is a broad category that includes both progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system as well as gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants. The progestogen subgroup comprises hormones that act as progesterone-like agents, regulating menstrual cycles, supporting pregnancy, and influencing genital tissue function. Gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants represent another subgroup that directly stimulate ovarian follicle development and ovulation, thereby modulating reproductive physiology. Together, these subcategories illustrate how various sex hormones and related modulators work in concert to control genital system function and fertility, underscoring their importance in reproductive health management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1276
retrieved_docs.content: Sex hormones and modulators of the genital system represent a broad therapeutic class that includes both hormonal contraceptives for systemic use and gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants as specific subcategories. Within this framework, hormonal contraceptives for systemic use are identified as a form of emergency contraception (pohotovostní antikoncepce). This hierarchical organization highlights how agents that alter reproductive hormone activity—ranging from systemic contraceptive formulations to ovulation‑stimulating gonadotropins—are grouped under the overarching category of sex hormones and genital system modulators, facilitating a clearer understanding of their roles in reproductive health management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1839
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist and Progestogens, endocrine therapy drugs are both classified as hormones and related agents. Within the category of Progestogens, endocrine therapy drugs, medroxyprogesteron is identified as a specific member, illustrating that medroxyprogesteron is a particular progestogenic agent used in endocrine therapy. This organization underscores that Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone Agonist and the broader class of progestogenic endocrine therapy drugs share a common hormonal classification, with medroxyprogesteron representing a concrete example of a progestogen employed in therapeutic regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2736
retrieved_docs.content: Choriogonadotropin is classified within the structural class of gonadotropiny, indicating that it belongs to the gonadotropin family of hormones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1644
retrieved_docs.content: Follitropin beta is classified within the structural class of gonadotropiny. Gonadotropiny represent a group of reproductive hormones that encompass the lidský choriogonadotropin (human chorionic gonadotropin). This classification indicates that follitropin beta shares key structural characteristics with the human chorionic gonadotropin family, underscoring its role in the regulation of gonadal function and its relevance in therapeutic applications that target the reproductive endocrine system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2772
retrieved_docs.content: Megestrol acetate is classified as a progestin, a synthetic analogue of the natural hormone progesterone, and is used in medical practice for hormone‑related therapies, including certain cancer treatments, hormone replacement, and appetite stimulation.
